title: Efficient Longest Increasing Subsequence Finder with Edge Case Handling

statement.markdown: Given an integer array `nums`, return the length of the longest increasing subsequence. An increasing subsequence is a subsequence of numbers whose every element appears exactly once and is smaller than the next one.
statement.inputFormat: Input is an integer array `nums` of size `n`.
statement.outputFormat: Return the length of the longest increasing subsequence.
statement.notes: Time complexity should be O(n^2), where n is the size of the input array.

hints.text: Consider using dynamic programming to solve this problem. Think about how to build up a table of lengths of increasing subsequences ending at each position.

editorial.title: Efficient Longest Increasing Subsequence Finder
editorial.intuition: The idea is to build up a table `dp` where `dp[i]` represents the length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index `i`. We can do this by iterating over the array and for each element, comparing it with all previous elements to see if it can form a longer increasing subsequence.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would be to try all possible subsequences and check if they are increasing.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use dynamic programming to build up the table `dp` in O(n^2) time.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use dynamic programming to build up the table `dp` in O(n^2) time.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the dynamic programming approach can be proven by showing that it correctly builds up the table `dp`.
editorial.dryRun: Example run of the dynamic programming approach to find the longest increasing subsequence.
